caveats: a: The occupational source is three-digit fabricated metal manufacturing.
a: The operating example is a much larger structural-steel fabricator and may overstate BIM sophistication.
rho: No representative adoption study for LMM ornamental-metal firms was found.
rho: The estimate assumes structured digital drawings and excludes autonomous shop or field work.
e: Eligibility is not observed for the injected firm list.
e: Some establishments may combine fabrication, erection, and adjacent structural-metal work across NAICS boundaries.
s5: The source measures plans, not completed qualifying transfers.
s5: Its population is broader and generally smaller than the LMM band.
q: Structural-steel project economics are only a proxy for ornamental and architectural metal.
q: No evidence directly measures AI savings pass-through.
d5: Construction spending is nominal and broader than the current service basket.
d5: Demand varies materially by geography and project type.
o: Operator persistence is inferred from physical fabrication and project accountability rather than observed directly.
o: Highly standardized railings or stairs may shift to catalog products more rapidly than bespoke work.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project-based B2B revenue under negotiated or competitively bid fixed-price contracts and purchase orders, with repeat general-contractor and institutional customer relationships, progress billing, and change orders for scope revisions.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive fabrication shops, field-only installers without manufacturing, commodity distributors, structural-metal activities classified elsewhere, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market contract manufacturers that repeatedly design-assist, detail, fabricate, and sometimes install architectural metal products such as staircases, railings, open steel flooring, fire escapes, and scaffolding for external contractors, architects, developers, and owners.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for all fabricated metal product manufacturing, not 332323, while DBM Global illustrates structural-steel detailing, BIM, estimating, fabrication, and project management at larger scale. I map portions of takeoff, estimating, CAD/BIM detailing, design coordination, procurement, scheduling, quality documentation, change-order support, and administration to AI-addressable tasks, leaving cutting, forming, welding, finishing, material handling, installation, and field measurement mostly unexposed. Custom architectural firms likely have more design and estimating labor than the broader BLS group, so the occupation proxy may bias exposure downward.
caveats: The occupational source is three-digit fabricated metal manufacturing.
The operating example is a much larger structural-steel fabricator and may overstate BIM sophistication.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task diaries from 15-20 LMM architectural-metal fabricators, split across estimating, detailing, project management, procurement, shop, field, quality, and administration and adjusted for current CAD/CAM automation, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: The workforce is production-heavy, but each custom project generates substantial drawing, takeoff, coordination, planning, and commercial work. Digital engineering and BIM are already important, making information-intensive tasks addressable while shop and field crafts remain physical.

d5.bridge: AIA forecasts nonresidential building spending down 0.3% in 2026 and up 3.0% in 2027, with highly uneven sector performance, but this is nominal construction spending rather than constant-quality demand for architectural metal. I assume weak near-term quantity followed by modest recovery, netting to about 0.4% annual growth over five years; price inflation in the spending forecast likely biases the proxy upward.
caveats: Construction spending is nominal and broader than the current service basket.
Demand varies materially by geography and project type.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year Dodge, ConstructConnect, or FMI square-footage and starts forecasts by building type, joined to fabricator backlog, bid volume, and metal intensity per project, would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Architectural metal follows renovation and new building work across commercial, institutional, residential, industrial, and infrastructure-adjacent projects. Data centers, healthcare, recreation, and selected public work support demand, while offices, manufacturing projects, financing costs, and tariffs create downside.

caveats: Eligibility is not observed for the injected firm list.
Some establishments may combine fabrication, erection, and adjacent structural-metal work across NAICS boundaries.
e.change_evidence: A coded census of the 424 injected LMM-band firms using manufacturing assets, external revenue, repeat-customer mix, installation share, ownership, and project type would directly establish eligibility.
e.rationale: The official product scope is inherently custom architectural metal, and repeat outsourced fabrication for external construction customers is common. Exclusions are mainly captive shops, field-only installers, misclassified distributors, and firms without a transferable customer and operating base.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 332323 ($3,094,475,067) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 332323 ($10,856,552,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $262,122 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 332323); class <5: 1,289 firms, avg revenue/firm $413,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $81,129 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 582 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,243,218 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $243,919 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 418 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,840,624 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $557,331 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 363 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,243,435 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,813,562 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 61 firms, avg revenue/firm $29,571,934 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,802,014 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 34 firms, avg revenue/firm $95,687,118 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $18,773,812 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 424

caveats: Operator persistence is inferred from physical fabrication and project accountability rather than observed directly.
Highly standardized railings or stairs may shift to catalog products more rapidly than bespoke work.
o.change_evidence: Contractor procurement interviews and project data quantifying modular catalog substitution, direct imports, self-performed fabrication, robotic cells, and design-to-machine workflows would materially change persistence.
o.rationale: Nearly all year-five quantity still requires an accountable fabricator to translate approved designs into code-compliant physical components, manage tolerances and materials, and often coordinate installation. Software can automate design and procurement, while standardized modular products, overseas sourcing, and contractor self-performance can displace a limited share of local operator work.

q.bridge: DBM Global is a large structural-steel fabricator rather than an LMM ornamental-metal firm, but it performs primarily under fixed-price contracts and uses digital engineering, BIM, design-assist, and fabrication. Fixed prices let the contractor retain within-project efficiency but subsequent competitive bids and negotiated budgets reset prices. Larger DBM may have more purchasing and bargaining power, biasing retention upward.
caveats: Structural-steel project economics are only a proxy for ornamental and architectural metal.
No evidence directly measures AI savings pass-through.
q.change_evidence: Bid-level estimates, awarded prices, labor budgets, actual hours, change orders, rebids, customer concentration, and margins before and after specific productivity deployments would materially change retained benefit.
q.rationale: Productivity gains on awarded fixed-price work can accrue to the fabricator, but contractors bid project by project and customers compare alternatives. As estimators learn new cost curves, lower bids, negotiated savings, and competitive pressure share benefits over the five-year window; change-order disputes can also dilute savings.

caveats: No representative adoption study for LMM ornamental-metal firms was found.
The estimate assumes structured digital drawings and excludes autonomous shop or field work.
rho.change_evidence: Before/after studies from representative fabricators measuring estimator hours, detailer throughput, drawing errors, RFIs, rework, bid hit rate, shop utilization, and gross margin would materially move implementation expectations.
rho.rationale: Document extraction, takeoffs, bid comparison, drawing checks, submittals, scheduling, purchasing, and project correspondence can be implemented within five years, especially around existing BIM/CAD systems. Customer-specific geometry, site conditions, code compliance, model coordination, fabrication tolerances, and change management require human review and integration discipline.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures U.S. employer-business owner intentions across industries; 22% plan a sale or transfer within five years. I use a slightly higher base because this fragmented trade has many owner-operated employer firms and transferable shops, crews, equipment, and contractor relationships, while bonding, owner dependence, gifts, failed processes, and internal transfers reduce qualifying completions. The industry adjustment may bias upward if key customer relationships are highly personal.
caveats: The source measures plans, not completed qualifying transfers.
Its population is broader and generally smaller than the LMM band.
s5.change_evidence: BizBuySell, Axial, PitchBook, construction M&A broker, SBA 7(a), and bonding-agent records for closed, failed, and internally transferred metal-fabrication businesses, paired with owner-age data, would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: The large modeled firm population and fragmented operating landscape create potential deal flow. Transferability varies with management depth, estimating discipline, backlog quality, safety record, bonding, and whether the owner personally holds customer relationships.

principal_driver: A high labor share and document-intensive custom fabrication workflow create meaningful implementable AI opportunity.
principal_weakness: Competitive project bidding and volatile construction demand limit durable commercial retention.

run_meta.title: Ornamental and Architectural Metal Work Manufacturing

sources.supports: Official activity definition for ornamental and architectural metal work including staircases, flooring, railings, and scaffolding, plus production and nonproduction role context.

sources.supports: Industrial AI opportunities and deployment barriers involving integration, data, reliability, explainability, and safety.
sources.title: 2026 Roadmap on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning for Smart Manufacturing

sources.supports: Current nonresidential construction conditions, 2026-2027 forecasts, sector divergence, and architecture-billings leading evidence.
sources.title: July 2026 Consensus Construction Forecast

sources.supports: U.S. employer-business five-year sale and transfer intentions.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

title: Ornamental and Architectural Metal Work Manufacturing
